title: Clotted cream fudge
subtitle: A very rich, smooth fudge made with clotted cream
excerpt: Clotted cream fudge uses clotted cream instead of condensed milk, giving a deeper, more buttery flavour and a slightly smoother, creamier set than standard fudge. It is cooked to soft-ball stage, beaten briefly as it cools, and set in a thin slab. The texture is richer and more fragile than condensed-milk fudge.
sourceNotes: Clotted cream fudge is a Devonshire and Cornish confectionery tradition using the region's abundant clotted cream in place of condensed milk. The formula is a regional variation on the standard beaten-fudge technique documented in British domestic confectionery from the late nineteenth century onward.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cut pieces in an airtight tin. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin at room temperature for 10 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: soft-ball stage, 116°C; the cream's fat content means it cooks slightly differently from condensed milk, so watch the thermometer carefully

content.text: Clotted cream fudge is standard fudge made with clotted cream in place of condensed milk. The clotted cream has already had most of its water driven off during manufacture, which means the mixture cooks more quickly and the result is richer and denser than condensed-milk fudge, with a slightly smoother, more melt-in-the-mouth texture. A sugar thermometer is necessary.
Sugar cooked above 100°C causes serious burns. If hot syrup touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed.

Combine the sugar, clotted cream, butter, and milk in a large, heavy saucepan. Heat gently, stirring, until the sugar has dissolved completely. Increase the heat to medium and clip a sugar thermometer to the side of the pan with the tip clear of the base. Cook, stirring frequently, until the mixture reaches 116°C (soft-ball stage). The clotted cream's high fat content causes the mixture to look darker and more amber than a condensed-milk version; this is normal.

Remove from the heat and stir in the vanilla. Leave to cool for 5 minutes without stirring. Then beat with a wooden spoon for 8 to 10 minutes until the fudge thickens and turns opaque; it requires less beating than condensed-milk fudge because the cream fat gives it a head start on setting. Pour into a buttered and lined 18 cm square tin. Leave at room temperature for 90 minutes until set firm. Cut into pieces with a warm knife.
